blocks.content: Shirone threw an Elixir to the merchant with the drone.
The merchant, receiving it with both hands, trembled.
He was so moved he could barely hold it properly.
It wasn't a Red, Green, or Yellow Elixir, but a White Elixir.
Flipping his palm, Shirone spoke.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: How about it? Is it settled now? Give Kanya the item.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: This won't do. That Elixir must also be mine for the deal to proceed.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Then take it from him.
The merchant's eyes twitched.
He realized Shirone's actions weren't mere provocations.
Shirone turned to the merchant fondling the Elixir and asked.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: So, what will you do? Will you give it back?

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: No, no! The deal cannot be canceled!
The merchant panicked, waving his hands.
Sure, the Mecha Tribe drone was valuable, but only if it was usable.
The drone he sold was practically worth only a Yellow Elixir. To exchange it for a White Elixir was a once-in-a-lifetime gain.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: So, what now? Are we going to have a fight among heretics? Seems to be your specialty.
The herbalist's face hardened.

This was indeed a tricky situation. He could be shameless in customer negotiations, but conflict among merchants brought no benefits. Especially for lower community merchants who mainly dealt in daily necessities.
The herbalist then scrutinized Shirone.
The way he pressured like this meant he was undoubtedly a Norin, and not an easy one.
Should he accept just one Elixir and move on? Trading Epinephrine for a White Elixir would be a legendary deal.
But it was a matter of pride. He didn't want to accept that things had gone the citizen's way.

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: Fine, I won't sell. So enjoy your toy. Do you realize you've crushed this girl's hopes?
The merchant had thrown in the towel. He chose the satisfaction of victory over profit.
As a merchant, it was a zero-point deal, but he didn't care as long as he could see the citizen despair.
Shirone knew he had won the game.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, you must sell the potion. Otherwise, you're violating the contract.

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: What? How am I violating any contract?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: You said you would sell the potion if I brought all my Elixirs. Kanya did that. Suddenly canceling the deal is a breach of contract. You know what happens to a merchant who loses credibility, right?
It wasn't just knowing; it was the end. At the very least, he would lose his foothold in the community.
But he also felt a bit wronged. Shirone was being stubborn.

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: Be clear. When I proposed the contract, you had two Elixirs. After the deal was made, you bought the drone, so I haven't violated anything.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: How do you know when I bought the drone?
The herbalist's eyes widened in shock.
Though he had lost his cool, his long experience in trading had given him a hunch.
He was at a disadvantage.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I already had a contract to buy the drone. I just hadn't handed over the Elixir yet. As a merchant, you should have confirmed that before making a deal. If you doubt it, feel free to check.
The herbalist ground his teeth.
His hunch was right.
The merchant who sold the drone would back Shirone no matter what. Proving his contract with Shirone meant the White Elixir's ownership would transfer.
This was a common occurrence on the mainland. He had no right to complain, having exploited monopolies himself.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Why am I at a disadvantage? It should be the opposite.
As he reviewed the situation, the herbalist realized Shirone had known the drone's lower value.
How Shirone figured it out was unclear, but without it, the current situation wouldn't exist.
The merchant sided with Shirone because he made several times the profit.
He let out an exclamation.
Shirone understood human nature's relationship with material goods. Even mainland merchants would find him formidable.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: So, what do you think? We had the first contract, right?

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: Of course, we did. We made a verbal agreement to trade the drone for a White Elixir before the girl started yelling.
Though unrequested, Shirone confirmed it directly.
Now, there was no way out. If he had a deal with another merchant, it was his fault for not checking the customer's limit.
Should he back down? One White Elixir was an incredible gain, but it was a matter of pride.
The herbalist played his lowest card as a merchant.
He went for self-pity.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: My mother was a citizen of Heaven. But she didn't undergo the ritual. Do you know why?
When no one answered, the herbalist continued, tapping his head.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: She went mad. My father was murdered in Heaven by a lunatic who had a crush on my mother. Do you know what punishment he got? Forty years off his lifespan. But he still had forty years left. My father was dead. My mother cursed that man all her life. But the ritual's date was set. Alongside the man who killed my father.
Kanya's eyes shook.
Shirone didn't grasp the significance, but everyone else was horrified.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: So, my mother killed herself. The ritual's target decreased, and I had to bear the remaining punishment. That's why I fled Heaven. So, do you get it? I hate citizens. They make me sick. Do you think I'll sell the potion?
In short, he was ready to blow everything up.
Shirone snorted.
A truly suicidal person wouldn't explain their reasons. If both parties would die, the process didn't matter.
What he wanted was an emotional victory, and Shirone wasn't going to give it to him.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: So, will you sell the potion or not?

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: I won't. And I won't give up the Elixir either. Do as you please.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Then I'll take it by force.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Do it. But remember, using violence means you can't stay in the community. You'll be a wanderer forever.
Shirone extended his hand.
Photon energy condensed into a vibrating white sphere.
The herbalist was terrified. Such magic was impossible. Only those contracting with the Spirit of Light from the community outskirts could do this.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Are you a border person?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The Vortex Serpent was quite strong. Thanks to it, I got three White Elixirs.
Shirone remained expressionless.
The herbalist knew that a mage was most ruthless when emotionless.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What kind of Elixirs might be in your body?
Everyone knew the herbalist's survival depended on his answer.
The herbalist struggled for a long time.
He realized he couldn't win.
Finally, he raised his hand with a bitter smile.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: None. I don't have any Elixirs in me. So spare me.
Shirone fired the Photon Canon.
A beam of light grazed the herbalist's nose and hit the ruins' pillar.
The pillar exploded with a loud noise.
The herbalist, now somber, looked at Shirone.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Don't laugh. You may be bluffing, but I'm serious. I don't care about the laws or the community. One more trick, and I won't forgive you.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Okay, okay. Calm down.
The herbalist realized the seriousness of the situation.
Shirone had shown maximum hostility without using violence. Like a swordsman drawing his blade or a beast baring its fangs, there would be no further leniency.
Understanding this, the herbalist threw Epinephrine to Kanya.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: There, satisfied? The deal is done.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: One more thing. Apologize to Kanya.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Why should I? You have no right to interfere with my personal feelings.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But you've been through pain, too, right?

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: What does that have to do with an apology?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I'm not asking for a meaningless apology. Words without sincerity are worthless. But didn't you want someone to understand your past wounds?

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Is that so? Have I still not escaped?
The herbalist glanced at Kanya's sorrowful face.
She said her mother was awaiting the ritual.
His mother had lost her mind back then. She screamed and self-harmed daily. The terror she felt was even more intense for him as a child.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Tch, who says I know how to apologize?
Snorting, the herbalist packed up his stall.
He tossed a purple potion to Kanya as he stood up.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: It's called Corfin. It strengthens the nervous system. Consider it a service since I made a good haul. Using it with Epinephrine might help. Though it'll be futile.
Walking towards the tent village, the herbalist glanced back at Shirone.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: And kid, everything I said was a lie. Beating me is impressive, but if you can't spot that, you'll get crushed on the mainland.
Arin communicated through the mental channel.

blocks.speaker: Arin
blocks.content: Shirone, should I check if it was a lie?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, it's fine.
Shirone watched the herbalist's retreating figure.
With a hint of sadness.
Nor's Haven.
A place where wanderers of Purgatory rested.
Perhaps what they left behind here were not their weary bodies but the scars in their hearts.
The eventful shopping concluded.
Shirone comforted Kanya, who couldn't hold back her tears.
Beside them, Rena pouted, watching over them.
Amy's group, excluding Shirone, began a discussion.
The items bought with the three White Elixirs were the Fire Spirit, the drone, and Epinephrine.
Even though it was the best outcome for the situation, it was hard not to feel like they had lost out.
Tess, in particular, couldn't hide her guilt.

blocks.speaker: Tess
blocks.content: I know I was the one who asked for it, but are we sure about this? There were items that could have been more useful in battle. And Epinephrine was outrageously overpriced.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: What's the big deal? We can hunt for more if needed. No point worrying about the past.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Rian's right. And monetary loss isn't the only loss. Shirone used the Elixirs most efficiently, and that's what matters.
Comforted by Amy, Tess pouted.

blocks.speaker: Tess
blocks.content: What good is efficiency when the situation was a mess? I support Shirone's choices, but if the herbalist hadn't pulled his tricks, we could have gotten the drone for half the price.
Canis smirked.

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: Amy didn't mean it like that. Shirone didn't lose out at all. He bought something that couldn't be obtained, even for all the White Elixirs.

blocks.speaker: Tess
blocks.content: What? Was the Fire Spirit that valuable?

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: No. The most valuable was Epinephrine.
Tess thought Canis was joking.
Even the thought of buying something that could be bought with Red Elixirs for a White Elixir was infuriating.
Rian asked in confusion.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: Why was Epinephrine the most valuable? It's a common potion.

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: True, but today its price was beyond imagination. Most importantly, its owners are Kanya and Rena, citizens of Heaven.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: Oh...

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: See? It wasn't about Epinephrine. Shirone bought a ticket to Heaven for a White Elixir.
Indeed.
Thinking back, it was a far-fetched idea to find a way into Heaven. But now they had a foothold. With Kanya's help, they could enter Heaven.
Shirone threw an elixir at the merchant who owned the drone.
The merchant's hands trembled as he caught it.
Overwhelmed with emotion, he could barely hold it properly.
It wasn't a red, green, or yellow elixir—it was a white elixir.
Shirone flipped his palm and spoke.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Well, that settles it, right? Now give Kanya the item.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: This isn't how it works. That elixir should also be mine for the trade to be valid.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Then try taking it from him.
The merchant's eyes twitched.
He realized Shirone's actions weren't just a simple provocation.
As if to prove it, Shirone turned to the merchant fiddling with the elixir and asked,

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: He's saying no. What are you going to do? Can you back down now?

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: N-no! The deal is absolutely not canceled!
The merchant panicked and waved his hands.
Of course, the mecha drone was valuable—but only if it was functional.
The drone he'd sold was practically worthless, barely worth a yellow elixir. To trade it for a white elixir was a once-in-a-lifetime profit.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: So, what now? Are you going to fight among yourselves? That seems to be your specialty.
The apothecary's face stiffened.

This was indeed an awkward situation. While he could be shameless in haggling with customers, conflicts between merchants were pointless. Especially among lower-tier community merchants who dealt in daily necessities.
Only then did the apothecary scrutinize Shirone carefully.
The way he applied pressure made it clear—he was a Nor. And not just any Nor.
Should he just take the one elixir and let it go? Trading Epines for a white elixir would go down in history as the deal of the century.
But it was a matter of pride. He didn't want to admit that a Nor had outplayed him.

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: Fine, I won't sell. So take your little toy and be satisfied. You know what? You just crushed this girl's hopes.
The merchant had drawn the line. He'd chosen the thrill of victory over profit.
As a merchant, it was a terrible deal, but seeing a Nor suffer was worth it.
Shirone realized he'd lost the game.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, you have to sell the medicine. Otherwise, you're breaking the contract.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: What? What contract did I break?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: You said it yourself. If I brought you all the elixirs I had, you'd sell the medicine. Kanya did that. But now you're canceling the deal—that's a breach of contract. Do you know what happens when a merchant loses credibility?
He knew—it was the end. At the very least, he'd never be able to do business in the community again.
But he couldn't help feeling wronged. Shirone was the one being unreasonable.

blocks.speaker: Herbalist
blocks.content: Get your facts straight. When I proposed the deal, you only had two elixirs. And since you bought the drone right after the deal was made, I didn't break anything.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: How do you know when I bought the drone?
The apothecary's pupils shook in shock.
Even in his irrational state, his years of merchant experience told him the truth.
He was the one at a disadvantage.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I already had a contract to buy the drone. I just hadn't handed over the elixir yet. For a merchant, you didn't even check that much before making the deal. You've got a long way to go. If you don't believe me, go ahead and verify it.
The apothecary gritted his teeth.
His instincts told him how this would play out.
The merchant who sold the drone would side with Shirone no matter what. Proving the contract with Shirone would transfer ownership of the white elixir.
This was commonplace in the homeland. He'd profited from monopolies himself, so he wasn't in a position to criticize.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: Why am I the one at a disadvantage? Shouldn't it be the other way around?
As he replayed the events, the apothecary realized something. Shirone had known the drone's value was lower than expected.
He didn't know how Shirone had figured it out, but if that wasn't the case, this situation wouldn't exist.
The merchant was siding with Shirone because he'd made several times the profit.
A sigh escaped him.
Shirone understood human nature when it came to material gain. This was the kind of skill that would thrive even among homeland merchants.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Well, old man? We had the contract first, right?

blocks.speaker: Merchant
blocks.content: Of course we did. Before that girl even started yelling, we'd already agreed verbally to trade the drone for the white elixir.
Without even being asked, Shirone had provided the confirmation.
Now there was no way out. If another merchant had a prior contract, it was the apothecary's fault for not verifying the customer's limits.
Should he back down now? One white elixir was already a huge gain, but it was a matter of pride.
He played the lowest card a merchant could.
He bared his fangs.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: My mother was a subject of Heaven. But she never drank the wine of tales. Do you know why?
When no one answered, the apothecary tapped his head and continued.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: She went mad. My father was murdered in Heaven—by some lunatic who was obsessed with her. And do you know what the punishment was? A reduction of 40 years from his lifespan. And yet, he still had 40 years left. My father died, but that man lived. My mother cursed him her entire life, kekeke. And then, absurdly, the date for the wine of tales was set—for her and that very same man who killed my father.
Kanya's eyes trembled.
Shirone didn't understand the significance, but everyone else was horrified.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: So my mother took her own life. With one less target for the wine's magic, the remaining debt fell to me. That's why I fled, escaping the law. Do you understand now? I hate subjects of Heaven. The sight of them makes me sick! And you think I'll sell you medicine?
In short, he was saying, 'Since we're both screwed, let's die together.'
Shirone snorted in response.
Someone truly resolved to self-destruct wouldn't explain their reasons. In a life-or-death situation, the process didn't matter.
What the apothecary wanted was an emotional victory, and Shirone had no intention of indulging him.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: So, are you going to sell the medicine or not?

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: I won't sell it. And I won't spit out the elixir either. I've made my decision, so do whatever you want.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Then I'll just take it by force.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: Go ahead. But are you sure that's wise? Use violence, and you'll never set foot in the community again. You'll be a wanderer for life.
Shirone extended his hand.
Photons compressed, forming a vibrating white sphere.
The apothecary was stunned. This kind of magic was impossible. The only ones who could wield it were those who'd contracted with light spirits—outsiders of the community.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: You… are you a Boundary Dweller?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The Vortex Serpent was quite strong. Thanks to it, I got three white elixirs.
Shirone showed no emotion.
And the apothecary knew—when mages suppressed their emotions, they became the most cruel and ruthless.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: What kind of elixir do you have in your body?
Everyone knew the apothecary's survival hinged on his answer.
The apothecary struggled for a long time.
This was rock bottom.
Realizing he couldn't win, he forced a bitter smile and raised his hands.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: None. There's no elixir in my body. So spare me.
Shirone fired the photon cannon.
A streak of light grazed the apothecary's nose and embedded itself in a ruin's pillar.
explosion
A deafening explosion shattered the pillar.
The apothecary stared at Shirone, all traces of laughter gone from his face.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: Don't laugh. You might be bluffing, but I'm serious. I don't care about the law or the community. If you try anything underhanded again, I won't forgive you.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Fine, I get it. I understand. Calm down.
The apothecary realized the gravity of the situation.
Shirone had shown the maximum hostility short of violence. For a swordsman, it was like drawing a blade; for a beast, baring fangs.
Knowing there would be no further concessions, the apothecary threw the Epines to Kanya.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: There, it's done. The deal is over.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: One more thing. Apologize to Kanya.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: Why should I? You have no right to interfere with my personal feelings.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But you have painful memories too, don't you?

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: What does that have to do with apologizing?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I'm not saying you have to apologize no matter what. Empty words are worthless. But didn't you also want someone to acknowledge your past wounds?

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: Was that true? Had he… never moved on?
The apothecary looked at Kanya's pitiful face.
She'd said her mother was waiting for the wine of tales.
Back then, his mother had been insane. She'd screamed every day and harmed herself. Her terror had only amplified his own as a child.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: Tch, who'd bother with an apology?
The apothecary snorted and began packing up his stall.
He stuffed the medicine bottles into his bag, rolled up the blanket, and slung it over his backpack.
As he dusted himself off, he tossed a purple potion to Kanya.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: It's called Corfin. A nervous system enhancer. Since I made a killing, I'll give it to you as a freebie. Use it with the Epines, and it'll have some effect. Not that it'll change anything.
As he walked toward the tent village, the apothecary glanced back at Shirone.

blocks.speaker: Apothecary
blocks.content: And kid, everything I said was a lie. You outplayed me, but if you can't even see through that much, you'll get crushed in the homeland.
Arin relayed a message through the mental channel.

blocks.speaker: Arin
blocks.content: Shirone, should I check if he's lying?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: No, it's fine.
Shirone watched the apothecary's retreating figure.
With a lonely gaze.
Nor's Haven.
A place where wanderers of purgatory could catch their breath.
Perhaps what they set down here wasn't just their weary bodies but the scars on their hearts.
The dazzling shopping spree had come to an end.
Shirone comforted Kanya, who was struggling to hold back tears.
Beside them, Rena puffed out her cheeks and watched.
Meanwhile, Amy's group began a meeting—without Shirone.
The items purchased with the three white elixirs were the fire spirit, the drone, and the Epines.
While it was the best choice under the circumstances, it was hard not to feel like they'd lost out.
Tess, in particular, couldn't hide her guilt.

blocks.speaker: Tess
blocks.content: It's not my place to say since I was the one pushing to buy it, but… are you really okay with this? There were things that could've helped in battle. Especially the Epines—we paid an absurdly high price for it.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: What does it matter? If we need more, we'll just hunt for it. No point dwelling on the past.

blocks.speaker: Amy
blocks.content: Rian's right. And it's not just a monetary loss. Shirone used the elixirs as efficiently as possible. If he's satisfied, that's all that matters.
Tess made a face at Amy's consolation.

blocks.speaker: Tess
blocks.content: What good is efficiency if the situation's a mess? I support Shirone's choice, but if that apothecary hadn't pulled his tricks, we could've haggled the drone down to half.
Kanis smirked and interjected.

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: That's not what Amy meant. Shirone didn't actually lose anything. He bought something we couldn't have gotten even if we'd spent all three white elixirs.

blocks.speaker: Tess
blocks.content: Huh? The fire spirit was that valuable?

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: No. The most expensive item was the Epines.
Tess thought Kanis, who wasn't even close to them, was joking.
But even as she said it, the thought of buying something obtainable with a red elixir for a white one made her blood boil.
Rian frowned in confusion.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: Why was the Epines the most expensive? It's just a common drug.

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: Normally, yes. But today, its value was beyond imagination. More importantly, it's about the owner. Kanya and Rena—subjects of Heaven.

blocks.speaker: Rian
blocks.content: Ah…

blocks.speaker: Canis
blocks.content: Now you get it? It's not about the Epines. Shirone used one white elixir to buy a ticket to Heaven.
Exactly.
Until now, finding a way into Heaven had seemed impossible. But now, they had a foothold. With Kanya's help, they could enter Heaven.
